facts.text: Stephen King writes 2,000 words every single day, including holidays and his birthday.
The word 'journal' comes from the French word 'jour,' meaning day.
Ernest Hemingway wrote standing up at a specially built desk in his home.
J.K. Rowling was rejected by 12 publishers before Bloomsbury accepted Harry Potter.
The longest novel ever written is 'In Search of Lost Time' by Marcel Proust, at roughly 1.2 million words.
Bullet journaling was invented by Ryder Carroll, a digital product designer from Brooklyn, New York.
Agatha Christie is the best-selling fiction writer of all time, with over 2 billion copies sold worldwide.
NaNoWriMo (National Novel Writing Month) challenges writers to produce 50,000 words in November.
Shakespeare invented over 1,700 words in the English language, including 'assassin,' 'lonely,' and 'generous.'
The first known diary in English was kept by Edward VI, who began writing it in 1547.
Mark Twain was the first major author to submit a typewritten manuscript to a publisher — 'Life on the Mississippi' in 1883.
Julia Cameron's 'The Artist's Way' popularized the practice of writing three pages of stream-of-consciousness every morning, called 'morning pages.'
Charles Dickens published most of his novels as weekly or monthly serial installments before they were collected into books.
The Pulitzer Prize for Fiction has been awarded since 1918.
A 'pantser' is a writer who writes by the seat of their pants without an outline, as opposed to a 'plotter' who outlines in advance.
Maya Angelou rented a hotel room to write in each day, removing all art from the walls so nothing would distract her.
The Bible is the most translated book in the world, available in over 700 languages.
F. Scott Fitzgerald's 'The Great Gatsby' sold poorly during his lifetime — only about 25,000 copies.
Anne Frank began writing her diary on June 12, 1942, her 13th birthday.
Toni Morrison won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1993, becoming the first African American woman to receive the honor.
Aaron Sorkin is famous for writing dialogue-heavy scripts, often composing while walking and talking out loud.
The Moleskine notebook brand was revived in 1997, inspired by the legendary notebooks used by Hemingway, Picasso, and Bruce Chatwin.
Edgar Allan Poe is credited with inventing the modern detective story with 'The Murders in the Rue Morgue' in 1841.
A standard Hollywood screenplay is about 90 to 120 pages long, with one page roughly equaling one minute of screen time.
Jane Austen published her novels anonymously, credited only as 'By a Lady.'
NaNoWriMo was founded in 1999 by freelance writer Chris Baty with just 21 participants in the San Francisco Bay Area.
The Nobel Prize in Literature has been awarded since 1901.
Victor Hugo reportedly wrote 'The Hunchback of Notre-Dame' by locking away his clothes so he couldn't go outside.
Self-publishing through Amazon Kindle Direct Publishing (KDP) allows authors to earn up to 70% royalties on ebook sales.
Robert Frost's poem 'The Road Not Taken' is one of the most frequently misinterpreted poems in American literature.
The haiku is a traditional Japanese poetry form consisting of three lines with 5, 7, and 5 syllables respectively.
Ernest Hemingway was born on July 21, 1899, in Oak Park, Illinois.
Gratitude journaling — writing down three things you're grateful for daily — has been shown in studies to improve mental well-being.
The Booker Prize, one of the most prestigious literary awards in English, has been awarded since 1969.
Quentin Tarantino wrote 'Pulp Fiction' in longhand in Amsterdam cafés using red and black notebooks.
Emily Dickinson wrote nearly 1,800 poems in her lifetime, but only about 10 were published while she was alive.
The first typewriter to be commercially successful was the Remington No. 1, introduced in 1873.
The Hugo Award for Best Novel has been given annually since 1953 and is voted on by members of the World Science Fiction Convention.
Roald Dahl wrote all his stories in a small hut at the bottom of his garden using only Dixon Ticonderoga pencils and yellow legal pads.
A query letter is a one-page pitch sent to literary agents to convince them to represent your manuscript.
Walt Whitman self-published 'Leaves of Grass' in 1855, even setting some of the type himself.
Stephen King was born on September 21, 1947, in Portland, Maine.
The term 'writer's block' was coined by psychoanalyst Edmund Bergler in 1947.
Sylvia Plath's 'The Bell Jar' was first published under the pseudonym Victoria Lucas.
Scrivener is one of the most popular writing software tools, designed specifically for long-form writing projects like novels and screenplays.
Samuel Pepys kept a detailed diary from 1660 to 1669, written in shorthand, providing a vivid account of life in Restoration London.
Mark Twain was born Samuel Langhorne Clemens on November 30, 1835.
A sonnet is a 14-line poem, traditionally written in iambic pentameter, popularized by Petrarch and Shakespeare.
The National Book Award has been given to American authors annually since 1950.
Fyodor Dostoevsky dictated 'The Gambler' in just 26 days to meet a publisher's deadline, with the help of stenographer Anna Grigoryevna Snitkina, whom he later married.
Fountain pens were first patented in 1827, though reliable versions didn't appear until the 1880s.
The limerick is a five-line humorous poem with an AABBA rhyme scheme, popularized by Edward Lear.
J.K. Rowling's birthday is July 31, the same birthday she gave to Harry Potter.
The world record for most books written by a single author is held by L. Ron Hubbard, with 1,084 published works.
Blogging began in the late 1990s, with the term 'weblog' coined by Jorn Barger in 1997.
Toni Morrison woke at 5 a.m. every day to write before going to her job as an editor at Random House.
The Nebula Award for Best Novel is given by the Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America.
Charles Dickens was born on February 7, 1812, in Portsmouth, England.
Writing by hand activates different parts of the brain than typing and has been shown to improve memory and comprehension.
Agatha Christie disappeared for 11 days in 1926, sparking a massive manhunt — she was later found at a hotel under a different name.
Amazon KDP was launched in November 2007 and has since become the largest platform for self-published ebooks.
Ray Bradbury wrote 'Fahrenheit 451' in nine days on a rented typewriter in the basement of the UCLA library.
The average traditionally published novel is between 70,000 and 100,000 words.
Maya Angelou was born Marguerite Annie Johnson on April 4, 1928.
The first Pulitzer Prizes were awarded on June 4, 1917, established by Joseph Pulitzer's will.
Jack Kerouac typed the first draft of 'On the Road' on a continuous 120-foot scroll of paper in just three weeks.
A writing retreat is a dedicated period where writers remove themselves from daily life to focus intensely on their work.
The Man Booker International Prize, created in 2005, recognizes a single book translated into English.
Jane Austen was born on December 16, 1775, in Steventon, Hampshire, England.
George Orwell's '1984' was published on June 8, 1949.
The phrase 'Kill your darlings' — meaning cut beloved but unnecessary prose — is often attributed to William Faulkner but likely originated with Arthur Quiller-Couch.
'Water for Elephants' by Sara Gruen started as a NaNoWriMo project and became a New York Times bestseller and major film.
Memoir writing differs from autobiography in that it focuses on a specific theme or period rather than the author's entire life.
The most expensive book ever sold at auction is Leonardo da Vinci's 'Codex Leicester,' purchased by Bill Gates for $30.8 million in 1994.
F. Scott Fitzgerald was born on September 24, 1896, in Saint Paul, Minnesota.
Anne Frank's diary has been published in over 70 languages and has sold more than 30 million copies worldwide.
Truman Capote claimed he could only write lying down, with a cigarette and a cup of coffee.
Google Docs has become a popular free writing tool because of its real-time collaboration and cloud-saving features.
The Brontë sisters — Charlotte, Emily, and Anne — initially published under the male pseudonyms Currer, Ellis, and Acton Bell.
Playwriting dates back to ancient Greece, with Aeschylus, Sophocles, and Euripides among the earliest known playwrights.
Edgar Allan Poe was born on January 19, 1809, in Boston, Massachusetts.
The average reading speed for an adult is about 200-250 words per minute.
Dan Brown's 'The Da Vinci Code' sold over 80 million copies worldwide and was translated into 44 languages.
Ernest Hemingway won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954 for 'The Old Man and the Sea.'
The practice of journaling has been linked to reduced stress, improved immune function, and better sleep.
Mary Shelley wrote 'Frankenstein' when she was just 18 years old, after a ghost story challenge during a rainy summer in Switzerland.
The New York Times Best Seller list has been published weekly since 1931.
Shakespeare was born on approximately April 23, 1564, in Stratford-upon-Avon.
A ghostwriter is a professional writer hired to write for someone else who is credited as the author.
Anthony Trollope wrote for exactly three hours each morning before going to his day job at the Post Office, producing 47 novels.
The shortest complete story ever written is often attributed to Hemingway: 'For sale: baby shoes, never worn.'
National Poetry Month is observed in April in the United States.
Journalism gets its name from the French word 'journal,' reflecting its origins as a daily record of events.
Robert Frost was born on March 26, 1874, in San Francisco, California.
The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed using movable type in Europe.
Isaac Asimov published or edited more than 500 books during his lifetime, covering nearly every category of the Dewey Decimal System.
Traditional publishing typically involves a literary agent who submits manuscripts to acquisitions editors at publishing houses.
Emily Dickinson was born on December 10, 1830, in Amherst, Massachusetts.
The Oxford English Dictionary took over 70 years to complete its first edition, published in 1928.
'The Diary of a Young Girl' by Anne Frank was first published on June 25, 1947.
A pen name or pseudonym allows authors to write under a different identity — Mark Twain, George Orwell, and Lewis Carroll all used pen names.
Hemingway's 'iceberg theory' of writing holds that the deeper meaning should be implicit, not stated outright.
The average advance for a first-time novelist from a traditional publisher is between $5,000 and $15,000.
Sylvia Plath was born on October 27, 1932, in Boston, Massachusetts.
NaNoWriMo has grown to include over 400,000 participants from around the world each November.
Alexandre Dumas employed a team of ghostwriters and collaborators, most notably Auguste Maquet, who helped write 'The Three Musketeers.'
The word 'novel' comes from the Italian 'novella,' meaning a new, short story.
Walt Whitman was born on May 31, 1819, in West Hills, New York.
Franz Kafka asked his friend Max Brod to burn all his manuscripts after his death, but Brod published them instead.
The Hero's Journey, outlined by Joseph Campbell, is one of the most widely used story structures in fiction and screenwriting.
A standard screenplay uses Courier 12-point font so that one page equals roughly one minute of screen time.
The Diary of Samuel Pepys is considered one of the most important primary sources for the English Restoration period.
Leo Tolstoy's wife Sophia hand-copied the manuscript of 'War and Peace' seven times.
The Newbery Medal, awarded for outstanding children's literature, has been given since 1922.
Agatha Christie was born on September 15, 1890, in Torquay, Devon, England.
The world's oldest known literary work is the 'Epic of Gilgamesh,' dating to around 2100 BCE in ancient Mesopotamia.
Haruki Murakami runs 10 kilometers or swims 1,500 meters every day as part of his writing discipline.
A writing prompt is a short scenario or question used to spark creativity and overcome writer's block.
Barbara Cartland holds the Guinness World Record for most novels published in a single year — 23 books in 1983.
The show-don't-tell principle encourages writers to convey emotions through actions and sensory details rather than direct statements.
The Caldecott Medal has been awarded annually since 1938 for the most distinguished American picture book for children.
Virginia Woolf argued in 'A Room of One's Own' that a woman must have money and a private space to write fiction.
The Dewey Decimal Classification system, used in libraries worldwide, was created in 1876 by Melvil Dewey.
Hunter S. Thompson used to retype entire novels by F. Scott Fitzgerald and Ernest Hemingway to learn the feel of great writing.
Creative nonfiction blends literary techniques with factual reporting, a genre popularized by writers like Truman Capote and Joan Didion.
The most translated author in the world is Agatha Christie, followed by Jules Verne and William Shakespeare.
James Patterson holds the record for the most #1 New York Times bestsellers by a single author, with over 100.
R.L. Stine's 'Goosebumps' series has sold over 400 million copies, making it one of the best-selling book series in history.
Screenwriter William Goldman coined the famous Hollywood saying, 'Nobody knows anything.'
World Poetry Day is observed annually on March 21.
The three-act structure — setup, confrontation, and resolution — has been the foundation of storytelling since Aristotle's 'Poetics.'
Maya Angelou published seven autobiographies, beginning with 'I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings' in 1969.
A first draft is sometimes called a 'zero draft' or 'vomit draft' because the goal is simply to get words on the page.
Leo Tolstoy was born on September 9, 1828, in Yasnaya Polyana, Russia.
The practice of freewriting — writing continuously without editing — was popularized by Peter Elbow in the 1970s.
Marcel Proust spent the last three years of his life confined to a cork-lined bedroom where he wrote and revised 'In Search of Lost Time.'
The average self-published author in the United States earns less than $1,000 per year from book sales.
Harper Lee published only one novel during her active career — 'To Kill a Mockingbird' in 1960 — before 'Go Set a Watchman' appeared in 2015.
The ISBN (International Standard Book Number) system was developed in the United Kingdom in 1966 and adopted internationally in 1970.
Beatrix Potter self-published 'The Tale of Peter Rabbit' in 1901 after being rejected by several publishers.
William Faulkner wrote 'As I Lay Dying' in six weeks while working night shifts at a power plant.
The world's largest library is the Library of Congress, holding over 170 million items.
Louisa May Alcott wrote 'Little Women' in just two and a half months, finishing in 1868.
J.R.R. Tolkien took 12 years to write 'The Lord of the Rings,' from 1937 to 1949.
The basic screenplay format includes scene headings (slug lines), action lines, character names, and dialogue.
George R.R. Martin is a famous 'gardener' writer, letting his stories grow organically rather than following strict outlines.
Oscar Wilde was born on October 16, 1854, in Dublin, Ireland.
A writer's residency provides dedicated time and space for authors, often at retreats like Yaddo, MacDowell, or Hedgebrook.
Donna Tartt spent 10 years writing 'The Goldfinch,' which won the 2014 Pulitzer Prize for Fiction.
The publishing industry uses the term 'backlist' to refer to books published in a prior season that are still in print and selling.
Charles Bukowski didn't publish his first novel until age 49, with 'Post Office' in 1971.
The villanelle is a 19-line poetic form with only two rhyme sounds, famously used in Dylan Thomas's 'Do Not Go Gentle into That Good Night.'
Nora Roberts is one of the most prolific romance writers, having published over 225 novels.
The world's first novel is widely considered to be 'The Tale of Genji' by Murasaki Shikibu, written around 1010 CE in Japan.
Mark Twain's 'Adventures of Huckleberry Finn' is often called the Great American Novel.
Virginia Woolf was born on January 25, 1882, in London, England.
An unreliable narrator is a storytelling device where the narrator's account is not entirely trustworthy.
John Steinbeck's dog ate the first draft of 'Of Mice and Men,' and Steinbeck described the manuscript as 'not bad.'
The Hemingway App is a popular online writing tool that highlights complex sentences and suggests simpler alternatives.
A chapbook is a small collection of poetry, typically 20 to 40 pages, often used as a first publication for emerging poets.
The 'Save the Cat' beat sheet, originally for screenwriting, is now widely used by novelists to structure their stories.
Diary writing has been practiced for centuries — Marcus Aurelius's 'Meditations' from the 2nd century CE is essentially a philosophical journal.
Jorge Luis Borges continued to write and dictate stories after going completely blind in his mid-fifties.
The average published book in the United States sells fewer than 500 copies.
Writing longhand can produce 15 to 30 words per minute, while the average typing speed is 40 to 50 words per minute.
World Book Day is celebrated on April 23 each year by UNESCO.
Aaron Sorkin wrote 'The Social Network' screenplay, which won him an Academy Award in 2011.
The National Novel Writing Month was expanded to include Camp NaNoWriMo sessions in April and July.
Romance novels make up a $1.4 billion industry and represent about 23% of the fiction market in the United States.
Jack London wrote at least 1,000 words every day, a discipline that helped him produce over 50 books.
The 'Snowflake Method' is a novel-planning technique that starts with a single sentence and expands outward into a full outline.
Gabriel García Márquez sold his car to support his family while writing 'One Hundred Years of Solitude.'
A literary agent typically takes a 15% commission on domestic book sales and 20% on foreign rights.
John Grisham was a full-time lawyer who woke at 5 a.m. to write 'A Time to Kill' over three years.
The shortest poem in the English language is often said to be 'Lines on the Antiquity of Microbes' by Strickland Gillilan: 'Adam / Had 'em.'
Herman Melville's 'Moby-Dick' was a commercial failure when published in 1851 and was largely forgotten until decades after his death.
Writing in second person — using 'you' as the protagonist — is rare but was famously used in Jay McInerney's 'Bright Lights, Big City.'
Terry Pratchett wrote over 40 Discworld novels, often composing on any available surface including napkins.
The concept of 'deep work,' as described by Cal Newport, is central to productive writing — extended periods of focused, distraction-free effort.
Maya Angelou won three Grammy Awards for her spoken word albums.
The Oxford comma (serial comma) is one of the most debated punctuation marks in the English language.
Kazuo Ishiguro won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2017 for novels of 'great emotional force.'
A logline is a one-sentence summary of a screenplay used to pitch the story to producers.
Toni Morrison was born Chloe Ardelia Wofford on February 18, 1931, in Lorain, Ohio.
Dr. Seuss's 'Green Eggs and Ham' uses only 50 different words, the result of a bet with his publisher.
The 'rule of three' in writing suggests that ideas presented in groups of three are more satisfying and memorable.
Freelance journalism has grown rapidly with the rise of online media, though pay rates have generally declined.
The first novel written on a typewriter is often said to be Mark Twain's 'The Adventures of Tom Sawyer' in 1876.
Colson Whitehead's 'The Underground Railroad' won both the Pulitzer Prize and the National Book Award.
In screenplay formatting, 'FADE IN:' is always the first text on page one and 'FADE OUT.' is the last.
Harper Lee was born on April 28, 1926, in Monroeville, Alabama.
Daily journaling for just 15 to 20 minutes has been shown to reduce symptoms of anxiety and depression.
The word 'plagiarism' comes from the Latin 'plagiarius,' meaning kidnapper.
Margaret Atwood wrote 'The Handmaid's Tale' in West Berlin in 1984, inspired by the proximity of the Berlin Wall.
The average query letter to a literary agent should be about 250 to 400 words.
William Shakespeare was baptized on April 26, 1564; his actual birth date is traditionally given as April 23.
A narrative arc typically follows five stages: exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, and denouement.
Stephen King's novel 'Carrie' was nearly thrown away before his wife Tabitha rescued the manuscript from the trash.
The PEN/Faulkner Award for Fiction has been given annually since 1981 and is the largest peer-juried literary award in America.
Journaling pioneer Ira Progoff developed the 'Intensive Journal' method in the 1960s as a therapeutic self-development tool.
The Lamy Safari and TWSBI Eco are among the most recommended fountain pens for writers who prefer writing by hand.
George Orwell's real name was Eric Arthur Blair, and he chose his pen name partly from the River Orwell in England.
The 'inciting incident' is the event that sets a story's main conflict in motion and typically occurs in the first act.
H.P. Lovecraft was a prolific letter writer, sending an estimated 100,000 letters during his lifetime.
Don Quixote by Miguel de Cervantes, published in 1605, is often cited as the first modern European novel.
A cliffhanger ending leaves a story unresolved, compelling the reader to continue — the term comes from Thomas Hardy's 1873 serial novel 'A Pair of Blue Eyes.'
The International Booker Prize (renamed in 2019) recognizes the best work of translated fiction published in the UK or Ireland.
Neil Gaiman writes his first drafts in fountain pen in notebooks, then types them up as a second draft.
Alice Walker won both the Pulitzer Prize and the National Book Award for 'The Color Purple' in 1983.
The flash fiction genre consists of very short stories, typically under 1,000 words, sometimes called 'sudden fiction' or 'micro fiction.'
'The Fools in Town Are on Our Side' by Ross Thomas was one of the first novels produced using a word processor.
Ayn Rand took 12 years to write 'Atlas Shrugged,' which she considered her magnum opus.
The concept of 'voice' in writing refers to the unique style and personality an author brings to their prose.
The Oprah Book Club, launched in 1996, had an enormous impact on book sales — an 'Oprah's pick' often guaranteed bestseller status.
Herman Melville dedicated 'Moby-Dick' to Nathaniel Hawthorne, writing 'In Token of My Admiration for His Genius.'
Travel writing is one of the oldest literary genres, dating back to Herodotus in the 5th century BCE.
Anne Rice wrote 'Interview with the Vampire' in just five weeks, partly as a way to cope with the death of her daughter.
The MFA (Master of Fine Arts) in Creative Writing is the terminal degree for writers in academia.
Charles Dickens popularized the serialized novel format in Victorian England, building suspense with weekly installments.
The Pomodoro Technique — writing in 25-minute focused bursts with short breaks — is popular among writers to maintain productivity.
Paulo Coelho's 'The Alchemist' was originally written in Portuguese and initially sold only one copy in the first week.
The collective noun for a group of writers is sometimes humorously called a 'block' of writers.
A palimpsest is a manuscript page that has been scraped clean and reused, reflecting the historical scarcity of writing materials.
Nora Ephron was a celebrated screenwriter known for 'When Harry Met Sally,' 'You've Got Mail,' and 'Sleepless in Seattle.'
The 'Ernest Hemingway' writing style is characterized by short sentences, simple words, and minimal adjectives or adverbs.
On average, a traditionally published book takes 18 to 24 months from manuscript acceptance to bookstore shelves.
George Eliot was the pen name of Mary Ann Evans, who used a male name to ensure her work would be taken seriously.
Kurt Vonnegut taught creative writing at the Iowa Writers' Workshop, one of the most prestigious MFA programs in the country.
The five-paragraph essay — introduction, three body paragraphs, and conclusion — is the most commonly taught writing format in American schools.
Dorothy Parker was known for her razor-sharp wit and was a founding member of the Algonquin Round Table.
An editor's role varies: developmental editors shape the story, line editors polish the prose, and copy editors fix grammar and consistency.
Grammarly, launched in 2009, has become one of the most widely used AI-powered writing assistance tools.
The longest-running newspaper comic strip that tells a continuous story is 'Gasoline Alley,' started in 1918.
T.S. Eliot worked as a bank clerk at Lloyd's Bank while writing 'The Waste Land,' one of the most important poems of the 20th century.
Many famous authors had day jobs: William Carlos Williams was a doctor, Wallace Stevens was an insurance executive, and Franz Kafka worked at an insurance company.
World Book and Copyright Day, observed on April 23, was proclaimed by UNESCO in 1995.
A writer's notebook or 'commonplace book' is a place to collect quotes, observations, and ideas — a tradition dating back to the Renaissance.
The Pulitzer Prize for Poetry has been awarded since 1922.
Roald Dahl was born on September 13, 1916, in Llandaff, Cardiff, Wales.
Ian Fleming wrote each of his 14 James Bond books during his annual two-month winter vacation at his Jamaica estate, Goldeneye.
Writing dialogue effectively requires each character to have a distinct voice that reflects their background and personality.
The concept of 'negative space' in writing means what's left unsaid can be as powerful as what appears on the page.
The Costa Book Awards (formerly the Whitbread Awards) have been given for literary excellence in the UK since 1971.
Zora Neale Hurston's 'Their Eyes Were Watching God' was rediscovered in the 1970s thanks to Alice Walker's advocacy.
A book proposal for nonfiction typically includes a query, overview, author bio, market analysis, chapter outline, and sample chapters.
Lewis Carroll's real name was Charles Lutwidge Dodgson, and he was a mathematics lecturer at Oxford University.
The 'Hero with a Thousand Faces' by Joseph Campbell inspired George Lucas to structure the original Star Wars trilogy.
Journaling before bed, especially writing a to-do list, has been associated with falling asleep faster in university studies.
The average word count of a Shakespearean play is about 22,000 words, with 'Hamlet' being the longest at approximately 30,557 words.
The concept of 'show, don't tell' is often traced back to Anton Chekhov, who advised writers to let details speak for themselves.
The Nobel Prize in Literature for 2016 went to Bob Dylan, marking the first time a songwriter won the award.
Cormac McCarthy didn't use quotation marks in his dialogue, a distinctive stylistic choice seen in 'The Road' and 'Blood Meridian.'
A manuscript page in standard format uses 12-point Courier font, double-spaced, with one-inch margins, yielding about 250 words per page.
John Keats wrote many of his greatest poems in a single year — 1819, including 'Ode to a Nightingale' and 'Ode on a Grecian Urn.'
Danielle Steel reportedly works 20 to 22 hours a day when writing and has published over 190 books.
The 'cold open' technique begins a story in the middle of action, hooking the reader before providing context.
Print-on-demand technology allows self-published authors to print books only when ordered, eliminating the need for large print runs.
The sonnet form has two main variations: the Petrarchan (Italian) sonnet with an octave and sestet, and the Shakespearean (English) sonnet with three quatrains and a couplet.
Scrivener was created by Keith Blount, who began developing it in 2004 because existing word processors didn't suit long-form writing.
Arthur Conan Doyle tried to kill off Sherlock Holmes in 'The Final Problem' (1893) but was forced by public demand to bring him back.
A sensitivity reader reviews manuscripts for potential biases or inaccurate portrayals of marginalized groups.
The phrase 'It was a dark and stormy night' from Edward Bulwer-Lytton's 1830 novel is regarded as the quintessential example of overwrought prose.
Amazon's Kindle was released on November 19, 2007, and sold out in five and a half hours.
Gabriel García Márquez was born on March 6, 1927, in Aracataca, Colombia.
The unreliable narrator technique was masterfully used by Vladimir Nabokov in 'Lolita' and Gillian Flynn in 'Gone Girl.'
Amanda Hocking became one of the first self-published millionaire authors, selling over 1.5 million ebooks by age 26.
A writing group or critique circle provides feedback on drafts and helps writers improve through peer review.
The concept of 'world-building' in fiction involves creating detailed settings with their own history, geography, and rules.
E.B. White's 'The Elements of Style' (co-authored with William Strunk Jr.) remains one of the most influential writing guides ever published.
The lyric essay blends poetry and nonfiction, using imagery and fragmentation rather than traditional narrative structure.
Philip Roth wrote standing at a lectern, walking around it as he composed, and estimated he walked half a mile for every page.
Fan fiction, where readers write stories using existing characters and worlds, has exploded online with platforms like Archive of Our Own (AO3).
The blank verse form — unrhymed iambic pentameter — was used extensively by Shakespeare and John Milton in 'Paradise Lost.'
A ghostwritten book still legally requires a contract specifying credit, payment, and copyright ownership.
The pen name 'Richard Bachman' was used by Stephen King early in his career to publish additional novels without market saturation.
The Writer's Market, published annually since 1921, is one of the oldest guides for writers seeking publishers and agents.
Flannery O'Connor wrote for two hours every morning without fail, producing some of the finest short stories in American literature.
The epistolary novel tells a story through letters, diary entries, or documents — examples include 'Dracula' by Bram Stoker and 'The Color Purple' by Alice Walker.
William Wordsworth and Samuel Taylor Coleridge published 'Lyrical Ballads' in 1798, launching the English Romantic poetry movement.
James Joyce spent 17 years writing 'Finnegans Wake,' a novel known for its experimental stream-of-consciousness style.
The concept of 'automatic writing' was used by the Surrealists to bypass the conscious mind and tap into the subconscious.
In screenwriting, a 'beat' is the smallest unit of dramatic action — a shift in behavior or intention that changes the dynamic of a scene.
Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie's TED talk 'The Danger of a Single Story' has been viewed over 30 million times and influenced writers worldwide.
The fountain pen company Montblanc named its flagship pen the 'Meisterstück' (Masterpiece), first introduced in 1924.
John Steinbeck won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1962.
A 'trunk novel' is a completed manuscript that an author decides not to publish, kept metaphorically in a trunk.
The Fibonacci poem (or Fib) uses the Fibonacci sequence to determine the number of syllables per line: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13...
Traditional publishers typically print hardcover editions first, followed by paperback editions 6 to 12 months later.
Virginia Woolf wrote most of her novels in the morning hours at a standing desk.
The 'Chekhov's gun' principle states that every element in a story should be necessary — if a gun appears on the wall in Act 1, it must be fired by Act 3.
Wattpad, founded in 2006, became one of the largest online platforms for user-generated stories, with over 90 million users.
A monologue in playwriting is an extended speech by one character, while a soliloquy is a speech made when the character is alone on stage.
Margaret Mitchell spent 10 years writing 'Gone with the Wind,' which won the Pulitzer Prize in 1937.
Anais Nin kept diaries from age 11 until her death at 73, producing one of the most extensive personal journals in literary history.
The 'rule of thumb' for dialogue tags is to use 'said' most of the time, as it becomes invisible to the reader.
The BookTok community on TikTok has significantly influenced book sales since 2020, reviving older titles and boosting debut authors.
A book's 'spine out' display in bookstores means only the spine is visible, making title and author name design critical.
Hemingway reviewed the ending of 'A Farewell to Arms' 47 times before he was satisfied.
The concept of a 'character arc' describes how a character changes over the course of a story.
Traditional Japanese calligraphy (shodo) is considered both a writing practice and an art form.
Screenplay spec scripts are written on speculation, without a buyer, in hopes of attracting interest from studios or producers.
The Library of Alexandria in ancient Egypt was one of the largest and most significant libraries of the ancient world.
In 2007, NaNoWriMo participant Erin Morgenstern drafted what would become 'The Night Circus,' published in 2011 to critical acclaim.
The concept of 'purple prose' refers to writing that is overly elaborate or ornate, detracting from the narrative.
A book advance is essentially a loan against future royalties — the author doesn't earn additional royalties until the advance 'earns out.'
Ralph Waldo Emerson kept a journal for nearly 50 years, filling over 200 volumes that formed the basis for many of his essays.
A 'meet cute' is a screenwriting term for the charming first encounter between romantic leads.
The Children's Literature Legacy Award (formerly the Wilder Award) honors an author or illustrator whose books have made a lasting contribution.
Shirley Jackson's 'The Lottery' caused the most mail The New Yorker had ever received for a story — most of it negative and outraged.
Stream of consciousness writing was pioneered by authors like Virginia Woolf, James Joyce, and William Faulkner.
A literary device is a technique used by writers to produce a special effect in their writing, such as metaphor, simile, or irony.
The National Spelling Bee has been held annually since 1925 in the United States.
Brandon Sanderson broke Kickstarter records in 2022 by raising $41.7 million for four secret novels.
The Oxford comma debate centers on whether to place a comma before 'and' in a list — style guides differ on the convention.
A beta reader is someone who reads a manuscript before publication and provides feedback from a reader's perspective.
World Storytelling Day is celebrated on March 20 each year.
Creative writing programs at universities became widespread after the Iowa Writers' Workshop was established in 1936.
The ancient Sumerians invented writing around 3400 BCE using cuneiform on clay tablets.
A blurb is a short promotional description on a book's back cover, designed to entice potential readers.
Daphne du Maurier wrote 'Rebecca' in 1938, one of the most beloved gothic novels in English literature.
Proofreading is the final stage of editing, focused on catching typos, formatting errors, and minor grammatical mistakes.
The average paperback novel ranges from 200 to 400 pages.
Ursula K. Le Guin spent over 50 years writing science fiction and fantasy and won multiple Hugo and Nebula Awards.
Ray Bradbury was born on August 22, 1920, in Waukegan, Illinois.
The 'in medias res' technique drops the reader into the middle of the action, a device used since Homer's 'Iliad.'
Post-it notes, introduced by 3M in 1980, became a favorite tool for writers organizing plot points and chapters.
An anthology is a collection of literary works chosen by a compiler, often themed around a genre, period, or subject.
The writing software Ulysses is popular among Mac users for its minimalist, distraction-free interface.
Hilary Mantel's 'Wolf Hall' trilogy took her over 20 years to complete and won the Booker Prize twice.
Flash nonfiction, also called the 'short short essay,' typically runs under 1,000 words and focuses on a single moment or idea.
The semicolon has become a symbol of mental health awareness, representing a sentence the author could have ended but chose not to.
Writing competitions like the Reedsy short story contest provide cash prizes and publication opportunities for emerging writers.
The Caldecott and Newbery Medals are announced during the American Library Association's Midwinter Meeting each January.
Arthur C. Clarke co-wrote the screenplay for '2001: A Space Odyssey' with director Stanley Kubrick.
Norman Mailer and Gore Vidal had one of the most famous literary feuds in American letters, spanning decades.
A writing portfolio showcases an author's best work and is often required when applying for writing jobs, MFA programs, or grants.
The phrase 'truth is stranger than fiction' is attributed to both Mark Twain and Lord Byron.
Using active voice — 'The dog bit the man' vs. 'The man was bitten by the dog' — generally makes prose more engaging and direct.
Louise Glück won the 2020 Nobel Prize in Literature, cited for 'her unmistakable poetic voice.'
The 'MacGuffin' is a plot device that serves no purpose other than to drive the story forward — a concept famously explained by Alfred Hitchcock.
A book tour traditionally involves an author traveling to bookstores across the country for readings and signings — now increasingly done virtually.
The epistolary format has evolved in modern fiction to include texts, emails, and social media posts.
NaNoWriMo's Young Writers Program allows students as young as kindergarten to set personalized writing goals.
The Writer's Digest has been published since 1920, making it one of the oldest magazines dedicated to the craft of writing.
The 'pathetic fallacy' is a literary device where nature mirrors the emotions of the characters — stormy weather during conflict, for example.
Octavia Butler was the first science fiction writer to receive a MacArthur Fellowship, in 1995.
The average American adult reads about 12 books per year, according to Pew Research Center surveys.
Chuck Palahniuk wrote 'Fight Club' in stolen moments during his day job as a diesel mechanic.
The term 'bestseller' was first used in the modern sense in 1889 by The Kansas City Star newspaper.
A 'deuteragonist' is the second most important character in a story, after the protagonist.
Langston Hughes was born on February 1, 1902, in Joplin, Missouri.
The average romance novel is between 50,000 and 100,000 words, depending on the subgenre.
Typewriter artist Paul Smith created intricate works of art using only the keys on his typewriter despite having severe cerebral palsy.
A 'red herring' in fiction is a misleading clue designed to distract the reader from the true solution.
The concept of 'death of the author,' coined by Roland Barthes in 1967, argues that the author's intentions should not determine a text's meaning.
Enid Blyton wrote approximately 762 books during her career, averaging about 10,000 words a day.
World Read Aloud Day is celebrated on the first Wednesday of February each year.
The sestina is a complex poetic form with six stanzas of six lines each, followed by a three-line envoi, using a rotating set of end words.
Zadie Smith was only 24 when her debut novel 'White Teeth' was published to critical acclaim in 2000.
Oscar Wilde wrote 'The Importance of Being Earnest' in just three weeks.
A 'deus ex machina' is when a seemingly unsolvable problem is suddenly resolved by an unexpected event — generally considered a flaw in writing.
National Handwriting Day is celebrated on January 23 each year, the birthday of John Hancock.
Manuscript literally means 'written by hand,' from the Latin 'manu scriptus.'
The Bechdel Test, originally from a comic strip by Alison Bechdel, is now widely used to evaluate female representation in fiction and screenwriting.
A 'plot hole' is an inconsistency in a storyline that goes against the logic established earlier in the narrative.
Dostoyevsky suffered from epilepsy, and his seizure experiences influenced the characters in 'The Idiot' and other novels.
The pen name 'Elena Ferrante' remains one of literature's greatest mysteries — the Italian novelist's true identity is still debated.
Writing in present tense creates a sense of immediacy, while past tense provides a sense of reflection and distance.
The Strand Magazine serialized Arthur Conan Doyle's Sherlock Holmes stories from 1891 to 1927.
Henry David Thoreau lived at Walden Pond for two years, two months, and two days, producing one of literature's greatest journals.
An acrostic poem spells out a word or phrase with the first letter of each line.
Philip Pullman writes 1,000 words per day, three pages of his writing pad, calling it his 'minimum daily word count.'
The 'Oxford Dictionaries Word of the Year' has been selected annually since 2004, reflecting language trends.
A denouement is the final resolution of a story's plot, after the climax and falling action.
Gloria Steinem co-founded Ms. Magazine in 1972, becoming one of the most influential voices in feminist journalism.
The longest English word without a repeating letter is 'uncopyrightable,' at 15 characters.
Jorge Luis Borges was born on August 24, 1899, in Buenos Aires, Argentina.
A writing 'voice' develops over time and is influenced by the author's reading habits, life experience, and practice.
The Amazon Associates program allows authors and bloggers to earn commissions by recommending books with affiliate links.
W.B. Yeats won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1923, the first Irishman to receive the honor.
A rhetorical question is a question asked for effect rather than to elicit an answer, commonly used in persuasive writing.
The Golden Age of Detective Fiction, from roughly 1920 to 1940, was dominated by writers like Agatha Christie, Dorothy L. Sayers, and Ellery Queen.
Audiobook sales have grown by over 25% annually since 2013, making narration an important consideration for authors.
Roberto Bolaño worked as a dishwasher, campground security guard, and garbage collector before becoming one of Latin America's most acclaimed novelists.
